story.title: Dr. Jackson and the Roller Coaster's Energy Chain
story.text:
Hi, I’m Dr. Brianna Jackson. At my lab, I study how energy moves and changes in systems. Think of me as an energy detective! We track how energy transforms from one form to another. Let's look at a roller coaster to see how this energy chain works.

1. At the top of the first big hill, the roller coaster car is full of Potential Energy. This is stored energy. The car has a lot of it because of its height and mass. It is waiting to be used.

2. As the car goes down the hill, that stored energy changes. The Potential Energy transforms into Kinetic Energy. Kinetic energy is the energy of motion. The car goes faster and faster as it falls.

3. At the bottom of the hill, the car has the most Kinetic Energy. But then it starts to slow down on the flat track. Where does that energy go? It doesn't just disappear. The law of Conservation of Energy says energy only changes form.

4. The energy changes again! Friction between the wheels and the track creates heat. This is Thermal Energy. This energy transformation from kinetic to thermal is why the car slows down. My job is to understand these chains to build better systems, like electric cars that recapture energy instead of losing it all as heat.
think_abouts: Where else do you see energy changing from stored to motion in your daily life?
What would happen to the roller coaster's speed if there was no friction?
story.go_deeper: Dr. Jackson's electric car uses 'regenerative braking' to turn kinetic energy back into stored electrical energy when it slows down. How is this different from the roller coaster losing energy to heat?

margin_defs.definition: A force that slows things down when they rub together.
To change from one thing into another.

check_prompts: At what point does the roller coaster have the most stored energy?

vocabulary.definition: Energy stored in an object due to its position or height. The higher it is, the more energy is stored.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A book on a high shelf has more potential energy than a book on the floor.

vocabulary.definition: Energy of motion. A moving object has kinetic energy based on its mass and speed.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A fast-moving baseball has a lot of kinetic energy.

vocabulary.definition: Heat energy. It is often generated by friction when surfaces rub together.
vocabulary.use_sentence: When you rub your hands together, you create thermal energy.

vocabulary.definition: The rule that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed from one form to another.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The law of conservation of energy explains why a bouncing ball eventually stops.

vocabulary.definition: The process of energy changing from one form to another, like potential to kinetic.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A flashlight shows energy transformation when chemical energy in the battery becomes light energy.

discussion_cards.prompt: Think about a time you went sledding or skateboarding. Describe where you had the most potential energy and where you had the most kinetic energy.
discussion_cards.frame: When I was at the top of the hill, I had the most potential energy because...

discussion_cards.prompt: How could you design a playground slide to have less friction? What would happen if you rode down it?
discussion_cards.frame: To make a slide have less friction, I could... This would make me go...

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Jackson studies energy chains. What is another example of an energy chain you can see in your school or home?
discussion_cards.frame: An energy chain I see is when...

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
